questions.text: A body of mass 2 kg is hung on a spring balance mounted vertically in a lift moving upward with an acceleration of 2 m/s². The reading on the spring balance will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is incorrect as it doesn't account for the acceleration of the lift.
This value is incorrect. The apparent weight is greater than the actual weight due to the lift's acceleration.

The reading on the spring balance in a lift includes both the force due to gravity and the apparent weight due to the acceleration of the lift. The apparent weight is given by m(g+a), where m is the mass of the object, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and a is the acceleration of the lift. Substituting the values, we get 2(9.8+2)=2×11.8=24kgm/s^2.
This value is incorrect as it doesn't account for the actual weight of the object.

questions.explanation: The reading on the spring balance in a lift includes both the force due to gravity and the apparent weight due to the acceleration of the lift. The apparent weight is given by m(g+a), where m is the mass of the object, g is the acceleration due to gravity, and a is the acceleration of the lift. Substituting the values, we get 2(9.8+2)=2×11.8=24kgm/s^2.

questions.text: A simple pendulum is made of a hollow sphere containing mercury suspended by means of a wire. If a little mercury is drained off, the period of the pendulum will:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because the period of a simple pendulum depends on its effective length.
This is incorrect because decreasing the effective length of the pendulum decreases its period.

Explanation: When mercury is drained off from the hollow sphere, the effective length of the pendulum decreases. As the period of a simple pendulum is directly proportional to the square root of its effective length, decreasing the length will decrease the period. Thus, the correct answer is option (c).

questions.text: The time period of a simple pendulum is T. If its length is increased by 4 times, its period will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because increasing the length of the pendulum affects the period.

The time period T of a simple pendulum is given by the formula T=2π(gL)^1/2​​, where L is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity. If the length of the pendulum is increased by 4 times, the new length becomes 4L. Plugging this into the formula, the new time period becomes T′=2π(4L/g)^1/2​​​​=2×2π(L/g)^1/2​​=2T.
This is incorrect because increasing the length of the pendulum typically increases the period.
This is incorrect because the period does not increase linearly with the length.

questions.explanation: The time period T of a simple pendulum is given by the formula T=2π(gL)^1/2​​, where L is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity. If the length of the pendulum is increased by 4 times, the new length becomes 4L. Plugging this into the formula, the new time period becomes T′=2π(4L/g)^1/2​​​​=2×2π(L/g)^1/2​​=2T.

questions.text: A heavily damped system has a fairly flat resonance curve in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Velocity is typically not directly plotted against time in resonance curves.

In a heavily damped system, the amplitude of the oscillation decreases rapidly, leading to a fairly flat resonance curve in distance or displacement versus time. This occurs because the damping force quickly dissipates the energy of the system, resulting in limited oscillation amplitude.
While acceleration is related to the dynamics of the system, it is not typically used to characterize resonance curves.
Amplitude-frequency resonance curves typically show the relationship between amplitude and frequency, but heavily damped systems do not exhibit pronounced resonant behavior in terms of amplitude.

questions.explanation: In a heavily damped system, the amplitude of the oscillation decreases rapidly, leading to a fairly flat resonance curve in distance or displacement versus time. This occurs because the damping force quickly dissipates the energy of the system, resulting in limited oscillation amplitude.

questions.text: A Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M) has amplitude A and time period T. Its maximum speed is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because it does not consider the factor of π, which is essential in the calculation of maximum speed in S.H.M.
This is incorrect because it represents the correct formula for maximum speed, but it fails to multiply by 2 to obtain the correct maximum speed.
This is incorrect because it does not consider the factor of π, which is essential in the calculation of maximum speed in S.H.M.

In Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M), the maximum speed Vmax​ is attained at the equilibrium position (mean position), where the acceleration is maximum. The maximum speed is directly proportional to the amplitude of the motion and inversely proportional to the time period. Therefore, Vmax​=2Πa/T​×2, which simplifies to 4πA/T​.

questions.explanation: In Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M), the maximum speed Vmax​ is attained at the equilibrium position (mean position), where the acceleration is maximum. The maximum speed is directly proportional to the amplitude of the motion and inversely proportional to the time period. Therefore, Vmax​=2Πa/T​×2, which simplifies to 4πA/T​.

questions.text: A loaded spring vibrates with period T. It is then cut into 4 equal parts, and the same load is suspended from one part. The new period is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because cutting the spring into 4 equal parts does not reduce the period to T/4​.
This is incorrect because cutting the spring into 4 equal parts increases the stiffness, which decreases the period beyond T/2​.

The period of vibration of a spring-mass system depends only on the mass and the spring constant, not on the amplitude or the load. When the spring is cut into 4 equal parts, the effective spring constant is increased by a factor of 4. This is because each part has the same load but only a quarter of the original length, so it will be 4 times stiffer. Therefore, the period T is halved, so the new period becomes 2T.
This is incorrect because cutting the spring into 4 equal parts decreases the period rather than increasing it.

questions.explanation: The period of vibration of a spring-mass system depends only on the mass and the spring constant, not on the amplitude or the load. When the spring is cut into 4 equal parts, the effective spring constant is increased by a factor of 4. This is because each part has the same load but only a quarter of the original length, so it will be 4 times stiffer. Therefore, the period T is halved, so the new period becomes 2T.

questions.text: The period of oscillation of a mass m suspended from a spring is T. Then the period of a mass 4m will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is incorrect because doubling the mass does not reduce the period to 1/4​ s.

The period of oscillation of a mass-spring system is given by T=2π(m/K)^1/2​​, where m is the mass and k is the spring constant. Since the period T is directly proportional to m/K​​, doubling the mass will increase the period by a factor of 2​. Therefore, the period for the mass 4m will be T/2​, or 1/2​ s.
This is incorrect because doubling the mass increases the period, so it's not 2 s.
This is incorrect because doubling the mass does not quadruple the period to 4 s.

questions.text: The acceleration of free fall on the moon is one-sixth of that on the earth. What would be the period on the moon of a simple pendulum which has a period of one second on the earth?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The period T of a simple pendulum is given by the formula T=2π(L/g)^1/2​​, where L is the length of the pendulum and g is the acceleration due to gravity. On the moon, the acceleration due to gravity (gm​) is one-sixth of that on the earth (ge​). Therefore, Tm​=2π(​L/gm)^1/2 ​​= 2π( ​L/1/6(ge))^1/2 =√6 ​×2π(​L/ge)^1/2 ​​=6​×Te​=√6x1 sec= √6 sec

questions.text: .The time period of a particle in simple harmonic motion is 8 seconds. At t = 0 it is at the mean position. The ratio of the distances travelled by it in the first and second seconds is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is the reciprocal of the calculated ratio (√2 - 1).
This is a common ratio in trigonometry, but it doesn't match the calculations for this specific scenario

Here's how to solve the problem:Identify relevant information:The time period (T) of the particle is 8 seconds.The particle starts at the mean position (equilibrium position) at t = 0.Formula for distance traveled in SHM:For a particle undergoing simple harmonic motion (SHM), the distance (d) traveled within a time interval (t) can be calculated using the following formula:d = A * |sin(ωt)|where:A is the amplitude of the motion (maximum displacement from the equilibrium position).ω (omega) is the angular frequency, which is related to the time period (T) by:ω = 2π / TFinding the angular frequency (ω):Using the formula: ω = 2π / T = 2π / 8 s = π/4 rad/sCalculating distances travelled in first and second seconds:Since the particle starts at the mean position, its initial displacement (x(0)) is 0. Therefore, we need to find the absolute values of sin(ωt) for t = 1 s and t = 2 s:Distance in first second (d₁):d₁ = A * |sin(π/4 * 1)| = A * |sin(π/4)| ≈ 0.707A (due to the positive square root, we consider the absolute value)Distance in second second (d₂):d₂ = A * |sin(π/4 * 2)| = A * |sin(π/2)| = A (since sin(π/2) = 1)Finding the ratio of distances (d₁/d₂):d₁/d₂ ≈ 0.707A / A = 0.707 ≈ √2 - 1Therefore, the ratio of the distances travelled by the particle in the first and second seconds is √2 - 1, which corresponds to option c
This value is significantly lower than the calculated ratio, which is closer to √2 than 1

questions.explanation: Here's how to solve the problem:Identify relevant information:The time period (T) of the particle is 8 seconds.The particle starts at the mean position (equilibrium position) at t = 0.Formula for distance traveled in SHM:For a particle undergoing simple harmonic motion (SHM), the distance (d) traveled within a time interval (t) can be calculated using the following formula:d = A * |sin(ωt)|where:A is the amplitude of the motion (maximum displacement from the equilibrium position).ω (omega) is the angular frequency, which is related to the time period (T) by:ω = 2π / TFinding the angular frequency (ω):Using the formula: ω = 2π / T = 2π / 8 s = π/4 rad/sCalculating distances travelled in first and second seconds:Since the particle starts at the mean position, its initial displacement (x(0)) is 0. Therefore, we need to find the absolute values of sin(ωt) for t = 1 s and t = 2 s:Distance in first second (d₁):d₁ = A * |sin(π/4 * 1)| = A * |sin(π/4)| ≈ 0.707A (due to the positive square root, we consider the absolute value)Distance in second second (d₂):d₂ = A * |sin(π/4 * 2)| = A * |sin(π/2)| = A (since sin(π/2) = 1)Finding the ratio of distances (d₁/d₂):d₁/d₂ ≈ 0.707A / A = 0.707 ≈ √2 - 1Therefore, the ratio of the distances travelled by the particle in the first and second seconds is √2 - 1, which corresponds to option c

questions.text: in case of vibrating pendulum, the potential energy is maximum at
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Understanding Potential Energy:Potential energy is the energy an object possesses due to its position in a conservative force field (like gravity in this case).As the object moves away from its equilibrium position (mean position), the potential energy increases because it stores more gravitational potential due to its increased height.Conversely, as the object moves closer to its equilibrium position, the potential energy decreases.Applying to Vibrating Pendulum:A vibrating pendulum oscillates about its mean position.At the extreme positions (maximum displacement from the mean position), the pendulum is farthest from its equilibrium point and therefore has the highest potential energy due to its maximum height relative to the mean position.As the pendulum swings towards the mean position, its potential energy gradually decreases until it reaches a minimum value at the mean position.

questions.explanation: Understanding Potential Energy:Potential energy is the energy an object possesses due to its position in a conservative force field (like gravity in this case).As the object moves away from its equilibrium position (mean position), the potential energy increases because it stores more gravitational potential due to its increased height.Conversely, as the object moves closer to its equilibrium position, the potential energy decreases.Applying to Vibrating Pendulum:A vibrating pendulum oscillates about its mean position.At the extreme positions (maximum displacement from the mean position), the pendulum is farthest from its equilibrium point and therefore has the highest potential energy due to its maximum height relative to the mean position.As the pendulum swings towards the mean position, its potential energy gradually decreases until it reaches a minimum value at the mean position.

questions.text: specific response of as system which is able to oscillateres with a certain period, to an external force acting with the same period is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: While the period is a characteristic of both the system and the external force for resonance to occur, it doesn't describe the specific response itself.
Wavelength is the distance between repeated points in a wave and doesn't directly relate to the system's response to the external force

When an external force has the same frequency (or period) as the natural frequency (or period) of an oscillating system, the system's amplitude of oscillation is significantly amplified. This phenomenon is known as resonance. During resonance, the external force synchronises with the system's natural vibrations, transferring energy efficiently and causing larger oscillations
This phenomenon describes the change in frequency or wavelength of a wave due to the relative motion between the source and the observer, not the specific response of a system to an external force

questions.explanation: When an external force has the same frequency (or period) as the natural frequency (or period) of an oscillating system, the system's amplitude of oscillation is significantly amplified. This phenomenon is known as resonance. During resonance, the external force synchronises with the system's natural vibrations, transferring energy efficiently and causing larger oscillations

questions.text: Which one of the following is a simple harmonic motion?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Wave moving through a string fixed at both ends.
options.explanation: In SHM, the motion is periodic, and the restoring force is proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position. A wave moving through a string fixed at both ends is an example of simple harmonic motion. The restoring force provided by the tension in the string is directly proportional to the displacement, and the motion is periodic and repetitive. This type of motion is commonly observed in various wave phenomena, such as water waves, sound waves, and electromagnetic waves.

options.text: Earth spinning about its own axis.
options.explanation: The Earth's rotation is not an example of simple harmonic motion. It involves rotational motion, but it does not exhibit the specific characteristics of SHM.

options.text: Ball bouncing between two rigid vertical walls
options.explanation: The motion of a ball bouncing between two walls is not simple harmonic motion. The bouncing motion involves repeated collisions and does not follow the criteria of SHM.

options.text: Particle moving in a circle with uniform speed.
options.explanation: This motion is not simple harmonic motion. It is circular motion with constant speed, and SHM involves oscillatory motion back and forth along a straight line.

questions.explanation: In SHM, the motion is periodic, and the restoring force is proportional to the displacement from the equilibrium position. A wave moving through a string fixed at both ends is an example of simple harmonic motion. The restoring force provided by the tension in the string is directly proportional to the displacement, and the motion is periodic and repetitive. This type of motion is commonly observed in various wave phenomena, such as water waves, sound waves, and electromagnetic waves.

options.text: Period remain same, and Energy becomes 4 times.
options.explanation: The period (T) of a simple harmonic oscillator is independent of its amplitude. The period is solely determined by the mass of the object and the force constant (spring constant for a spring) according to the formula:T=2π√m/kWhere T is the period,m is the massand k is the force constant.Doubling the amplitude of a simple harmonic oscillator does not affect its mass or force constant, and therefore, the period remains the same.The energy (E) of a simple harmonic oscillator is proportional to the square of its amplitude according to the equation:E=1/2​kA2Where E is the energyk is the force constantA is the amplitude.If the amplitude is doubled, the energy becomes four times its original value. ​​

questions.text: If length of simple pendulum is increased four times what will be effect on its frequency?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The frequency of a simple pendulum is given by:f = 1 / (2π) * √(g/l)where:f is the frequencyg is the acceleration due to gravityl is the length of the pendulumIf the length of the pendulum is increased four times, the new frequency will be:f' = 1 / (2π) * √(g/4l)f' = 1 / (2π) * √(g/l) * 1/2f' = f / 2Therefore, the frequency will be halved.

questions.explanation: The frequency of a simple pendulum is given by:f = 1 / (2π) * √(g/l)where:f is the frequencyg is the acceleration due to gravityl is the length of the pendulumIf the length of the pendulum is increased four times, the new frequency will be:f' = 1 / (2π) * √(g/4l)f' = 1 / (2π) * √(g/l) * 1/2f' = f / 2Therefore, the frequency will be halved.

questions.text: Acceleration of a body executing SHM is directed towards:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the acceleration of the body is always directed towards the mean position. This is because the restoring force, which is responsible for the oscillatory motion, is always directed towards the equilibrium position.

questions.text: A block of mass m = 2 kg is attached to an ideal spring of spring constant k = 200 N/m. The block is at rest at its equilibrium position. An impulsive force acts on the block, giving it an initial speed of 2 m/s. Find the amplitude of the resulting oscillations.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The total energy of the system is conserved. Initially, the block has only kinetic energy, which is converted into potential energy when it reaches the maximum displacement (amplitude).Initial kinetic energy = 1/2 * m * v^2 = 1/2 * 2 * 2^2 = 4 JMaximum potential energy = 1/2 * k * A^2 = 4 JSolving for the amplitude A:A = √(2 * 4 / k) = √(8 / 200) = 0.2 mTherefore, the amplitude of the resulting oscillations is 0.2 m.

questions.explanation: The total energy of the system is conserved. Initially, the block has only kinetic energy, which is converted into potential energy when it reaches the maximum displacement (amplitude).Initial kinetic energy = 1/2 * m * v^2 = 1/2 * 2 * 2^2 = 4 JMaximum potential energy = 1/2 * k * A^2 = 4 JSolving for the amplitude A:A = √(2 * 4 / k) = √(8 / 200) = 0.2 mTherefore, the amplitude of the resulting oscillations is 0.2 m.

questions.text: According to Hooke's law:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Hooke's law states that the restoring force (F) exerted by a spring is directly proportional to the displacement (x) from its equilibrium position. The proportionality constant is the spring constant (k). Mathematically, this is represented as:F = -kxThe negative sign indicates that the restoring force is always directed opposite to the displacement.

questions.text: If T is to be increased to 3T then length of pendulum should be increased by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time period of a simple pendulum is given by: T = 2π√(L/g)where:T is the time periodL is the length of the pendulumg is the acceleration due to gravityIf we want to increase the time period to 3T, we need to increase the length of the pendulum by a factor of 9. This is because the time period is proportional to the square root of the length. 3T = 2π√(L'/g)where L' is the new length of the pendulum.Dividing the second equation by the first equation, we get:basic 3 = √(L'/L)Squaring both sides, we get:basic 9 = L'/LTherefore, the length of the pendulum should be increased by 9 times to increase the time period to 3T.

This is because the time period is proportional to the square root of the length. 3T = 2π√(L'/g)where L' is the new length of the pendulum.Dividing the second equation by the first equation, we get:basic 3 = √(L'/L)Squaring both sides, we get:basic 9 = L'/LTherefore, the length of the pendulum should be increased by 9 times to increase the time period to 3T.

questions.text: If T is to be reduced to T/3 then length of pendulum should be:

This is because the time period is proportional to the square root of the length. T/3 = 2π√(L'/g)where L' is the new length of the pendulum.Dividing the second equation by the first equation, we get: 1/3 = √(L'/L)Squaring both sides, we get: 1/9 = L'/LTherefore, the length of the pendulum should be reduced to L/9 to reduce the time period to T/3.

questions.text: The question is: Second's pendulum has frequency equal to?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A second's pendulum takes two seconds to complete one full oscillation. This means its time period (T) is 2 seconds.Therefore, the frequency (f) is:f = 1/T = 1/2 = 0.5 Hz

questions.text: The question is: If length of the pendulum is halved time period will be?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time period of a simple pendulum is given by:T = 2π√(L/g)where:T is the time periodL is the length of the pendulumg is the acceleration due to gravityIf the length of the pendulum is halved, the new length becomes L/2. Let's denote the new time period as T'.T' = 2π√((L/2)/g)T' = 2π√(L/2g)T' = √(1/2) * 2π√(L/g)T' = (1/√2) * TT' = T/√2Therefore, the new time period (T') is T/√2, which is the same as T divided by the square root of 2.

questions.text: The question is: If the length of pendulum is increased 4 times, the new time period will be?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time period of a simple pendulum is given by:T = 2π√(L/g)where:T is the time periodL is the length of the pendulumg is the acceleration due to gravityIf the length of the pendulum is increased 4 times, the new length becomes 4L. Let's denote the new time period as T'.T' = 2π√((4L)/g)T' = 2π√(4L/g)T' = 2π√4 * √(L/g)T' = 2 * 2π√(L/g)T' = 2TTherefore, the new time period (T') is 2T, which is twice the original time period (T).

Let's denote the new time period as T'.T' = 2π√((4L)/g)T' = 2π√(4L/g)T' = 2π√4 * √(L/g)T' = 2 * 2π√(L/g)T' = 2TTherefore, the new time period (T') is 2T, which is twice the original time period (T).

questions.text: If a hole is dug to the center of earth and a ball is dropped in it, the ball will:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a ball is dropped into a hole dug to the center of the earth, it will experience a force due to gravity that is proportional to its distance from the center of the earth. This means that the force is always directed towards the center of the earth. As the ball falls, the force of gravity will decrease until it reaches zero at the center of the earth. At this point, the ball will have reached its maximum velocity. However, the ball will not stop at the center of the earth. Instead, it will continue to move due to its inertia. As the ball moves past the center of the earth, the force of gravity will start to increase again, pulling the ball back towards the center. This will cause the ball to slow down until it comes to a stop at the other end of the hole. The ball will then start to fall back towards the center again. This process will repeat itself, causing the ball to oscillate back and forth through the hole.

options.text: Continue its motion till the other end and stop

This will cause the ball to slow down until it comes to a stop at the other end of the hole. The ball will then start to fall back towards the center again. This process will repeat itself, causing the ball to oscillate back and forth through the hole.

questions.text: If the value of g is made g/16, what will be the effect on time period of pendulum?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time period of a simple pendulum is given by the formula: T = 2π√(L/g)where:T is the time periodL is the length of the pendulumg is the acceleration due to gravityIf the value of g is made g/16, then the time period will become: T' = 2π√(L/(g/16)) = 2π√(16L/g) = 4 * 2π√(L/g) = 4TTherefore, the time period of the pendulum will become four times the original time period.

questions.text: A vibrator produces 25 waves in 5 seconds. What is its time period?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time period of a wave is the time taken for one complete oscillation or cycle. It is the reciprocal of the frequency.Here, the vibrator produces 25 waves in 5 seconds. Therefore, the frequency of the vibrator is:Frequency (f) = Number of waves / Time = 25 waves / 5 seconds = 5 HzNow, the time period (T) is:Time period (T) = 1 / Frequency (f) = 1 / 5 Hz = 1/5 seconds

questions.explanation: The time period of a wave is the time taken for one complete oscillation or cycle. It is the reciprocal of the frequency.Here, the vibrator produces 25 waves in 5 seconds. questions.text: A simple pendulum with spring constant 500n/m oscillates between two points x1 = 10cm and x2 = 15 cm. What is total mechanical energy at 10cm?

options.explanation: The total mechanical energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is constant and is given by: E = 1/2 kA^2where:E is the total mechanical energyk is the spring constantA is the amplitude of oscillationThe amplitude is the maximum displacement from the equilibrium position. In this case, the amplitude is:apache - x1) / 2 = (15 cm - 10 cm) / 2 = 2.5 cm = 0.025 mTherefore, the total mechanical energy at 10 cm is: E = 1/2 * 500 N/m * (0.025 m)^2 = 1562.5 J

questions.text: If the length of simple pendulum is increased to 6 times and g reduces to 3 times, then time period will be?

options.explanation: The time period of a simple pendulum is given by: T = 2π√(L/g)where:T is the time periodL is the length of the pendulumg is the acceleration due to gravityIf the length of the pendulum is increased to 6 times and g is reduced to 3 times, then the new time period T' will be: T' = 2π√(6L/3g) = 2π√(2L/g) = √2 * 2π√(L/g) = √2 * TTherefore, the time period is increased by √2 times, which is approximately equal to √18 times.

questions.text: The total energy of a pendulum is 50J. What will be its kinetic energy at the mean position?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: At the mean position, the pendulum's velocity is maximum, and its potential energy is minimum (zero). Therefore, the total energy at the mean position is entirely kinetic energy.Energy Conservation in SHM:The total mechanical energy of a simple harmonic oscillator (like a pendulum) remains constant throughout its motion. This means that the sum of its kinetic energy (KE) and potential energy (PE) is always equal to the total energy (E):E = KE + PEAt the mean position, PE = 0. Therefore, the kinetic energy at the mean position is equal to the total energy:KE = E = 50J

questions.explanation: At the mean position, the pendulum's velocity is maximum, and its potential energy is minimum (zero). Therefore, the total energy at the mean position is entirely kinetic energy.Energy Conservation in SHM:The total mechanical energy of a simple harmonic oscillator (like a pendulum) remains constant throughout its motion. This means that the sum of its kinetic energy (KE) and potential energy (PE) is always equal to the total energy (E):E = KE + PEAt the mean position, PE = 0. Therefore, the kinetic energy at the mean position is equal to the total energy:KE = E = 50J

questions.text: If the length of a pendulum is 4 times g, its time period will be

options.explanation: The time period (T) of a simple pendulum is given by the formula:T = 2π√(L/g)where:L is the length of the pendulumg is the acceleration due to gravityIn this case, the length (L) is 4 times g. Substituting this into the formula:T = 2π√(4g/g)T = 2π√4T = 2π * 2T = 4π

questions.text: If spring constant is 2 and displacement is also 2 then sum of KE and PE?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The potential energy stored in a spring is given by:PE = (1/2) * k * x^2where k is the spring constant and x is the displacement.The kinetic energy of a spring is given by:KE = (1/2) * m * v^2where m is the mass of the object attached to the spring and v is its velocity.The total energy of the system is the sum of the potential energy and the kinetic energy.Total energy = PE + KEIn this case, the spring constant k = 2 and the displacement x = 2. So, the potential energy is:PE = (1/2) * 2 * 2^2 = 4 JSince the object is at rest, its velocity is zero, and therefore the kinetic energy is zero.KE = 0Therefore, the total energy of the system is:Total energy = PE + KE = 4 J + 0 J = 4 J

So, the potential energy is:PE = (1/2) * 2 * 2^2 = 4 JSince the object is at rest, its velocity is zero, and therefore the kinetic energy is zero.KE = 0Therefore, the total energy of the system is:Total energy = PE + KE = 4 J + 0 J = 4 J

questions.text: The general equation of SHM is given as:x(t) = A sin(wt)Find the approximate value of wt, if x(t) = 71cm and A=100cm
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The general equation of SHM is given as:x(t) = A sin(wt)where:x(t) is the displacement of the object at time tA is the amplitude of the oscillationw is the angular frequencyt is the timeWe are given that x(t) = 71cm and A = 100cm. Substituting these values into the equation above, we get:71 = 100 sin(wt)sin(wt) = 71/100 = 0.71To find the approximate value of wt, we need to find the angle whose sine is 0.71. Using a calculator, we find that the angle is approximately 45 degrees.Therefore, the approximate value of wt is 45 degrees.

questions.text: What is the kinetic energy at the extreme position during SHM?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the kinetic energy is maximum at the mean position and zero at the extreme positions. This is because at the extreme positions, the velocity of the object is zero, and kinetic energy is directly proportional to the square of velocity

questions.text: In Damping, what happens to the energy of the system?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Damping is a phenomenon where energy is gradually lost from an oscillating system due to frictional forces. These forces, like air resistance or internal friction, convert the mechanical energy of the system into other forms of energy, such as heat. This energy dissipation causes the amplitude of the oscillations to decrease over time.

questions.text: If the spring is halved, the new spring constant of each part will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The spring constant (k) is a measure of the stiffness of a spring. It is defined as the force required to stretch or compress the spring by a unit length.When a spring is halved, its length is reduced by half. However, the total amount of material in the spring remains the same. This means that the spring constant of each half will be doubled to maintain the same total spring constant.Therefore, if the original spring constant is k, the new spring constant of each half will be 2k. Since there are two halves, the total spring constant remains the same.

questions.text: Which of the following changes must when a wave changes its medium?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a wave travels from one medium to another, its speed changes. This is because the speed of a wave is dependent on the properties of the medium through which it is traveling. For example, the speed of sound is faster in solids than in liquids, and faster in liquids than in gases. The frequency of a wave, however, remains constant when it changes mediums.The relationship between the speed of a wave, its frequency, and its wavelength is given by:Copyv = fλwhere:v is the speed of the wavef is the frequency of the waveλ is the wavelength of the waveWhen a wave changes mediums, its wavelength changes to maintain a constant frequency. This change in wavelength leads to a change in the speed of the wave.

This change in wavelength leads to a change in the speed of the wave.

questions.text: In SHM _ is conserved
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the total mechanical energy of the system remains constant. This is because the energy is continuously transformed between kinetic energy (K.E) and potential energy (P.E) without any loss due to friction or other dissipative forces

questions.explanation: In Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM), the total mechanical energy of the system remains constant. This is because the energy is continuously transformed between kinetic energy (K.E) and potential energy (P.E) without any loss due to friction or other dissipative forces

questions.text: What will be impact on the frequency of a simple pendulum if the length of pendulum is halved and value of gravity is doubled?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The frequency of a simple pendulum is given by: f = 1 / (2π) * √(g/L)where:f is the frequencyg is the acceleration due to gravityL is the length of the pendulumIf the length of the pendulum is halved (L/2) and the value of gravity is doubled (2g), the new frequency will be: f' = 1 / (2π) * √(2g / (L/2))Simplifying the equation: f' = 1 / (2π) * √(4g / L) f' = 2 * (1 / (2π) * √(g / L)) f' = 2fTherefore, the frequency of the pendulum will be doubled.

questions.explanation: The frequency of a simple pendulum is given by: f = 1 / (2π) * √(g/L)where:f is the frequencyg is the acceleration due to gravityL is the length of the pendulumIf the length of the pendulum is halved (L/2) and the value of gravity is doubled (2g), the new frequency will be: f' = 1 / (2π) * √(2g / (L/2))Simplifying the equation: f' = 1 / (2π) * √(4g / L) f' = 2 * (1 / (2π) * √(g / L)) f' = 2fTherefore, the frequency of the pendulum will be doubled.

questions.text: In S.H.M which of the following remains constant?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In Simple Harmonic Motion, the total energy of the system remains constant. This is because the energy is continuously being converted between kinetic energy (K.E) and potential energy (P.E).Total energy (E) = K.E + P.EAt the extreme position, the velocity of the particle is zero, so K.E = 0. Therefore, the total energy is equal to the potential energy at the extreme position.At the mean position, the velocity of the particle is maximum, so K.E is maximum. Therefore, the total energy is equal to the kinetic energy at the mean position.The potential energy at the mean position is zero.The potential factor (P.F) is the ratio of potential energy to total energy.P.F = P.E / EAt the extreme position, P.F = 1.At the mean position, P.F = 0.Therefore, the total energy, potential factor at extreme position, and kinetic energy at the mean position remain constant in Simple Harmonic Motion.

questions.explanation: In Simple Harmonic Motion, the total energy of the system remains constant. This is because the energy is continuously being converted between kinetic energy (K.E) and potential energy (P.E).Total energy (E) = K.E + P.EAt the extreme position, the velocity of the particle is zero, so K.E = 0. Therefore, the total energy is equal to the potential energy at the extreme position.At the mean position, the velocity of the particle is maximum, so K.E is maximum. Therefore, the total energy is equal to the kinetic energy at the mean position.The potential energy at the mean position is zero.The potential factor (P.F) is the ratio of potential energy to total energy.P.F = P.E / EAt the extreme position, P.F = 1.At the mean position, P.F = 0.Therefore, the total energy, potential factor at extreme position, and kinetic energy at the mean position remain constant in Simple Harmonic Motion.

questions.text: If mass attached to a spring and the spring constant of a spring are doubled, the resulting Time period will:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time period of a spring-mass system is given by:T = 2π√(m/k)where m is the mass and k is the spring constant.If the mass and spring constant are both doubled, then the new time period T' will be:T' = 2π√(2m/2k) = 2π√(m/k) = TTherefore, the time period will remain the same

questions.explanation: The time period of a spring-mass system is given by:T = 2π√(m/k)where m is the mass and k is the spring constant.If the mass and spring constant are both doubled, then the new time period T' will be:T' = 2π√(2m/2k) = 2π√(m/k) = TTherefore, the time period will remain the same

Therefore, the kinetic energy (KE) will be zero in all these cases.
